# Overview

Schemata provides a unified cache abstraction (`ICacheProvider`) in `Schemata.Caching.Skeleton` — a pure contract with no framework dependency. It supports both simple key-value storage and collection (set) semantics, and is consumed by multiple subsystems: entity query caching, resource idempotency, authorization rate limiting, and tenancy caching.

Two concrete providers ship in-box, selectable at the composition root.

## ICacheProvider

```csharp
public interface ICacheProvider
{
    Task<byte[]?> GetAsync(string key, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task SetAsync(string key, byte[] value, CacheEntryOptions options, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task RemoveAsync(string key, CancellationToken ct = default);

    Task CollectionAddAsync(string key, string member, CacheEntryOptions options, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task<IReadOnlyList<string>?> CollectionMembersAsync(string key, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task CollectionRemoveAsync(string key, ICollection<string> members, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task CollectionRemoveAsync(string key, string member, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task CollectionClearAsync(string key, CancellationToken ct = default);
}
```

### Key-value operations

| Method        | Purpose                                   |
| ------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| `GetAsync`    | Retrieves raw bytes, or `null` if absent  |
| `SetAsync`    | Stores bytes with expiration options      |
| `RemoveAsync` | Deletes the entry (key-value or collection) |

### Collection operations

Collections simulate Redis-style set semantics. A collection is a set of string members stored under a single key. Members are added, enumerated, individually removed, or the entire collection is cleared.

| Method                   | Purpose                                              |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| `CollectionAddAsync`     | Adds a member to the set; creates the set if needed  |
| `CollectionMembersAsync` | Returns all members, or `null` if the set is absent  |
| `CollectionRemoveAsync`  | Removes one or more members from the set             |
| `CollectionClearAsync`   | Deletes the entire set                               |

Collections are used by the entity query cache's [reverse index](../repository/caching.md#reverse-index), where each set member is a cache key associated with a specific entity. The eviction advisors enumerate and delete these members on update/remove.

## CacheEntryOptions

```csharp
public class CacheEntryOptions
{
    public DateTimeOffset? AbsoluteExpiration { get; set; }
    public TimeSpan? AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow { get; set; }
    public TimeSpan? SlidingExpiration { get; set; }
}
```

| Property                           | Behavior                                      |
| ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| `AbsoluteExpiration`               | Expire at a specific UTC point in time        |
| `AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow`  | Expire after a fixed duration from storage    |
| `SlidingExpiration`                | Extend lifetime on each access                |

When multiple values are set, the earliest expiration takes effect. Both providers normalize `AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow` into an absolute expiration at storage time.

## Cache key format

All cache keys generated by Schemata use the same `ToCacheKey` extension method:

```csharp
string.Concat(SchemataConstants.Schemata, "\x1e", domain, "\x1e", CityHash.CityHash128(key))
```

The `SchemataConstants.Schemata` GUID prefix prevents collisions between different Schemata applications sharing a cache backend.

### Key domains

| Domain          | Constant              | Use                                |
| --------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `entity`        | `Keys.Entity`         | Entity/query cache keys, reverse index |
| `resource`      | `Keys.Resource`       | Resource create idempotency        |
| `authorization` | `Keys.Authorization`  | Device authorization polling       |
| `tenancy`       | `Keys.Tenancy`        | Tenant resolution cache            |

Each subsystem uses its own domain, so keys from different concerns never collide even if they hash the same plaintext.

CityHash128 is used for its speed and low collision rate on short- to medium-length keys.

## Consumers

`ICacheProvider` is resolved from DI by these built-in consumers:

| Consumer                                       | Domain             | Purpose                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| `AdviceQueryCache` / `AdviceResultCache`       | `Keys.Entity`      | Entity query result caching                          |
| `AdviceUpdateEvictCache` / `AdviceRemoveEvictCache` | `Keys.Entity` | Entity mutation cache eviction via reverse index     |
| `AdviceCreateRequestIdempotency` / `AdviceResponseIdempotency` | `Keys.Resource` | AIP-155 create idempotency              |
| `AdviceDeviceCodePolling`                      | `Keys.Authorization` | RFC 8628 device polling rate limiting               |

The provider is registered as a **singleton**. Consumers (advisors) are scoped.

## Providers

Two concrete implementations ship in-box:

| Package                          | Backend                     | Collection mechanism               | Multi-process |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------- |
| `Schemata.Caching.Distributed`   | Any `IDistributedCache`     | JSON `HashSet<string>` + in-process lock | Single-process only |
| `Schemata.Caching.Redis`         | Redis via `StackExchange.Redis` | Native Redis Set commands      | Cluster-safe  |

See [Cache Providers](providers.md) for registration, behavior details, and selection guidance.

## Registration

Register an `ICacheProvider` implementation during application startup. Choose one backend:

```csharp
// Option A: In-memory (single-process)
services.AddDistributedMemoryCache();
services.AddDistributedCache();

// Option B: Redis (multi-process, cluster-safe)
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IConnectionMultiplexer>(
    ConnectionMultiplexer.Connect("localhost:6379"));
builder.Services.AddRedisCache();
```

Both use `TryAddSingleton`, so an earlier `ICacheProvider` registration takes precedence.
